name: Remo Mobility
alexConclusions:
Overall, Remo is heavily influenced by Mota-Engil in how they conduct their business. They take very complex engineering projects or financially-heavy projects, because most likely Mota-Engil subsidizes them.

They have a strong push for destination charging and also transitioning special purpose fleets into EVs.

Most likely, because of how much they invest in the infra, and that they promise revenue sharing, they are looking for locations with high traffic, but also low monthly cost for parking slots, so that's why they are pushing the agenda that you will be “making money” as a site host.

Also, they most likely sign long term contracts with fleet owners, and have a high monthly retainer there.

Because they invest so much in the infra, and they don't have that many use-cases, most likely they want to optimise the CPMS cost tremendously, as it's not seen that much of a benefit, but as a minimum requirement to get their business rolling.

We also don't see too much roaming.

If I want to win them as a client I would make sure I have the following:

  • All regulations and public requirements in their countries are met
  • Also low monthly fee/low transaction fee
  • Promise them high utilisation — maybe through roaming and partnerships
  • Also, show them how they can provide flexible plans for the different types of destinations, as they want to have all the tools to attract lucrative destinations

Overall, it's not a client that uses software as leverage much. They come from engineering, and so their business feels more like financial services in a sense or engineering projects. Every running cost will be scrutinized heavily if I had to guess. Also, any obstacles to gain lucrative projects, will be seen as a no-go.

Most likely, Ampeco can cover most of these and unless there is serious friction when it comes to pricing, I am not expecting that there will be any meaningful opportunity any time soon. It will probably take another few years for them to grow enough that operations and fees stack up enough to re-evaluate again.

businessType.label: Category
businessType.value: Full CPO — backed by Mota-Engil engineering/construction group

businessType.label: Charging scope
businessType.value: Public destination + fleet (buses, trucks, logistics, municipal)

businessType.label: Business model
businessType.value: Zero-investment for site hosts and fleet clients — Remo/Mota-Engil funds the infrastructure

businessType.label: Fleet scope
businessType.value: eBuses, eTrucks, logistics, last-mile, waste collection, taxi, public transport

overview.countriesNote: Portugal primary market. Spain confirmed — ultra-fast hub installed in Benalmádena. Expansion likely tied to Mota-Engil project pipeline.

network.roamingNote: Roaming is present but not prominently promoted — consistent with a destination/fleet-focused CPO where drivers are expected to use the dedicated app.

partnerships.label: Mota-Engil
partnerships.value: Parent group — funds infrastructure, provides engineering capability and financial backing

partnerships.label: Kempower
partnerships.value: Ultra-fast hardware partner — confirmed at Benalmádena hub (Spain)

partnerships.label: Ampeco
partnerships.value: CPMS platform — confirmed via app and Ampeco case study

app.label: Reviews
app.value: Very few public reviews — insufficient data

app.label: Plug & Charge
app.value: Planned — part of their "convenience charging" vision

product.appNote: App is Ampeco-powered. Remo selected Ampeco specifically for flexibility, product roadmap transparency, the ability to influence roadmap, and white-label branding to build direct EV driver relationships.

stack.label: Fleet mgmt
stack.value: Fleet transition training programmes included in offer

siteHost.label: Target hosts
siteHost.value: Municipalities, golf courses, marine parkings, hotels, hospitals, restaurants, unused land

siteHost.label: Model
siteHost.value: Zero upfront cost — Remo invests; site host earns revenue share

siteHost.label: Host revenue
siteHost.value: Up to €100 per parking space claimed

siteHost.label: Long-term
siteHost.value: Remo takes the risk; likely locks in long-term revenue-share contracts

revenue.label: Session fees
revenue.value: Per-kWh or per-session charges from public and fleet users

revenue.label: Fleet retainers
revenue.value: Long-term monthly contracts for fleet-as-a-service clients

revenue.label: Rev-share sites
revenue.value: Remo retains majority; site host receives share

revenue.label: Financing
revenue.value: Financed infrastructure solutions — clients pay over time rather than upfront

funding.label: Parent
funding.value: Mota-Engil Group — major Portuguese engineering/construction group

funding.label: Model
funding.value: Mota-Engil subsidises infrastructure investment — gives Remo ability to offer zero-cost to clients

funding.label: Implication
funding.value: Every running cost (including CPMS) is scrutinised — the financial model is capex-heavy, not software-first

policy.label: Decarbonisation
policy.value: Core mission — positioned as fleet transition enabler, not just charger installer

policy.label: Public sector
policy.value: City councils and municipalities are active clients — public tender experience

salesSignals.text: Already an Ampeco client. This is known intel — use carefully. The relevant question is whether they are happy, what gaps remain, and whether they're approaching a renewal or expansion conversation.
Heavy capex model means every recurring software cost is under pressure. Ampeco pricing vs alternatives will be compared at renewal. Understanding their CPMS spend vs value perceived is key.

Fleet-as-a-service is complex. Managing eBus, eTruck, and last-mile fleets alongside public destination charging is a real multi-use-case challenge. Gaps in reporting, energy management, or billing may emerge as they scale.
Plug & Charge is in their roadmap — they chose Ampeco partly for this. If implementation has been slow, that's a pain point to probe.

openQuestions: How satisfied is Remo with Ampeco — are there unmet requirements or roadmap delays?
When is their Ampeco contract up for renewal?
Has Plug & Charge been delivered? What other roadmap items are pending?
How is their fleet-as-a-service billing working in practice — any operational gaps?
Is Mota-Engil expanding Remo into new markets, and what CPMS requirements does that create?

synthesis.verdictText: Remo is an existing Ampeco client — confirmed from app and case study. Not a near-term opportunity unless there is friction with Ampeco, a contract renewal approaching, or a new market expansion that creates new CPMS requirements. Monitor for signals of dissatisfaction or growth that outpaces current capabilities.

hypotheses.label: Hypothesis 1 — Cost scrutiny will intensify as they scale
hypotheses.text: Mota-Engil funds Remo's infrastructure but watches running costs closely. As the network grows from ~700 to thousands of charge points, CPMS fees become a more significant line item. A compelling cost-per-connector story would resonate at renewal.

hypotheses.label: Hypothesis 2 — Fleet-as-a-service complexity is the future gap
hypotheses.text: Remo's fleet offer is ambitious — eBuses, eTrucks, logistics, last-mile, all with financed infrastructure. As this scales, multi-depot energy management, complex billing, and driver reimbursement will stress a standard CPMS. Worth tracking if Ampeco keeps pace.

uncertainties: Ampeco relationship depth and satisfaction level unknown.
Contract length and renewal timeline unknown.
Unclear how much of Remo's fleet-as-a-service is live vs pipeline — actual operational complexity may be lower than projected.

contacts.name: Remo leadership
contacts.title: CEO / Head of Technology
contacts.note: Identify via LinkedIn — look for the person who owns the Ampeco relationship (likely CTO or Head of Product/Operations).

synthesis.contactsNote: Approach only when there is a concrete trigger — contract renewal, new market entry, or a product gap surfaced via market intel. Cold outreach without a hook is low ROI given they already have a CPMS.
